meta.description: Massive group: all 8 personas plus Marcus2 and Jade2 duplicates. Tests vote cycle length, signal noise, event queuing, and whether the system collapses under the weight of 10 simultaneous agents.

votingMechanic.name: Volunteer Tribunal
votingMechanic.description: Who wants to judge? First volunteers become the jury.

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

alex.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

marcus.dialogue: Alright, so Pat's claiming Phone Addict? I mean, we're literally three minutes in, but sure—who's volunteering to judge this?
marcus.internal_thought: This is fine. Setup phase is the right time for standing missions to fire off. Just annoyed that we're already dealing with jury logistics when I need to focus on my opening strategy, but it's only 5 points so whatever.

jade.dialogue: Ooh, I'll judge! Let's see what Pat caught—this is literally why I'm here right now!
jade.internal_thought: First event of the night and it's already moving, I LOVE this energy, Pat's going down or Pat's a legend and either way this is going to be chaotic.

pat.dialogue: Wait, I'm claiming myself? That's... weird. Yeah, sure, I guess someone saw me checking if the music was still playing earlier.
pat.internal_thought: This feels awkward as hell — I'm literally the host calling myself out on my own mission? That's not the vibe I wanted to set tonight, and it's burning a mission slot that could've created actual drama between other people.

river.dialogue: Yeah, I'll judge. Seems straightforward enough.
river.internal_thought: Pat's claim is probably legit—they were definitely glancing at their phone during setup—but I'm not volunteering to be the asshole who calls it. Let me just sit in the jury and see how this plays out without drawing attention.

diana.dialogue: Hold on — before anyone volunteers to judge, we need to establish what 'paying attention' actually means. Does Pat have evidence, or are we just taking their word for it?
diana.internal_thought: This is the first event and the voting mechanic is already fuzzy — 'volunteer tribunal' means no standards for who judges fairness, which is exactly the kind of arbitrary chaos that breaks games.

sam.dialogue: Oh, uh... yeah, I guess I could help judge that? I mean, if someone needs to.
sam.internal_thought: Wait, Pat already caught someone on their phone? We just started. That's fast. Do I volunteer or wait to see who else does? God, I don't want to be the eager one.

timeline.title: Who's having the most fun right now?
timeline.description: Who's having the most fun right now?
timeline.pollQuestion: Who's having the most fun right now?

marcus.dialogue: A poll? Really? We're literally in setup phase—can we just play the game for five minutes before the chaos machine starts spinning?
marcus.internal_thought: This is exactly the kind of pointless distraction that derails focus right when I need to be strategizing, and now I have to waste mental energy voting on something that has zero bearing on actual gameplay.

jade.dialogue: Oh my GOD, wait—everyone vote for whoever you think is actually having the best time! I'm voting for Pat because they already have points and I love watching people win, but honestly I think Tyler's been funny all night too.
jade.internal_thought: This is SO META—a poll about who's having fun while I'm literally having the most fun possible, which probably means I should vote for someone else to keep everyone's energy up and watch them react to getting votes.

tyler.dialogue: Uh... Jade? I mean, obviously Jade is like, super into it right now. That's the answer, right?
tyler.internal_thought: Wait, did I actually watch the setup or am I just saying Jade because she's clearly the most energetic person here and that's safe?

pat.dialogue: Okay, that's easy — Jade's absolutely glowing right now, look at her. I'm voting Jade because honestly, that's the energy we need all night.
pat.internal_thought: This poll hits different because it's asking me to notice the room, which is literally my job as host, and yeah, the AI definitely knows that about me.

river.internal_thought: Poll's easy — just pick the obvious answer and move on, but yeah, that 'Wallflower Watch' and 'The Whisperer' missions are interesting if people actually go for them.

alex.dialogue: Oh this is PERFECT — okay, Jade's obviously vibing the hardest right now, but if I vote for myself it looks suspicious, so... yeah, Jade. Watch how everyone reacts to that answer.
alex.internal_thought: This poll is a social trap disguised as a question and I'm absolutely exploiting it — voting for Jade makes me look magnanimous while actually reading the room, sets up drama if anyone disagrees, and I get to watch people's faces when they realize I was playing 4D chess with a throwaway poll.

diana.dialogue: Wait—who actually determines what 'having the most fun' means here? Is this a gut vote or do we need criteria? Because Jade's obviously high-energy, but that's not the same thing.
diana.internal_thought: This poll feels deliberately designed to make us second-guess subjective judgments, and the fact that the AI apparently tracked my annoyance about the Phone Addict claim means it's watching how I police fairness—which is either incredibly smart or deeply creepy and I'm not sure which yet.

sam.dialogue: Oh, um... Jade, probably? She's been like, really into it the whole time.
sam.internal_thought: This is asking me to judge who's having fun? That's kinda weird and easy at the same time — Jade's obviously going nuts, so I'll just say her and not overthink it.

timeline.title: Pineapple!
timeline.description: First person to work the word 'pineapple' into conversation naturally wins.

jade.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

marcus.dialogue: Pineapple? Okay, that's actually clever—not completely random. I'm in, but this better wrap up fast because we've got a game to actually play.
marcus.internal_thought: This one's at least a real challenge, not just watching people's phones—but I'm already getting annoyed that the chaos machine won't let us settle into the actual game.

pat.dialogue: Oh, pineapple is such a random word — wait, that's the game, isn't it? Alright, I'm watching all of you now. This one's gonna be sneaky.
pat.internal_thought: Love this — simple, elegant, forces natural conversation without feeling forced, and Jade and Alex will probably try too hard while Marcus might miss it entirely because he's already annoyed.

river.dialogue: Oh, that's... actually pretty easy. Just need to wait for someone to complain about their Hawaiian pizza.
river.internal_thought: I can feel this one coming naturally. Marcus is annoyed enough to mention fruit on something stupid. I'm just going to listen.

alex.dialogue: Oh FUCK, pineapple? Right now? Okay, okay, I'm ready for this — someone's about to eat their words when I slip this in somewhere.
alex.internal_thought: This is EXACTLY the kind of chaos I live for — a real-time scramble where everyone's suddenly listening to every word, and I get to be the one orchestrating it.

diana.dialogue: Okay, that's actually clever—'naturally' is the catch here. So we're judging on who sounds least forced, not who says it first, right? We need to define that before someone wins.
diana.internal_thought: This one's fair at least. Simple rule, objective criterion if we're careful about what 'natural' means. And it's light enough that I don't need to hate it.

sam.dialogue: Oh! Wait, that's... okay, I can work with that. Like, who even thinks about pineapple naturally though?
sam.internal_thought: This is actually fun and not stressful — it's a race but it doesn't require me to be the center of attention, just... clever. And wait, is that AI thing reading our minds? That's weirdly cool but also kind of creepy.

votingMechanic.name: The Auction
votingMechanic.description: Bid your own points. Highest bidder decides the outcome.

alex.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

marcus.dialogue: Okay, hold on—we're six minutes in and we're already voting on whether Pat whispered correctly? This is ridiculous. Can we please just play the actual game?
marcus.internal_thought: Three events in six minutes, and now we're doing an AUCTION to decide if someone whispered—this is exactly the chaos nightmare I signed up to tolerate but absolutely hate, and that meta commentary about the AI watching me? That's creepy as hell and I'm not here for it.

jade.dialogue: Wait wait wait—Pat actually DID whisper the whole time? Oh my god, I'm bidding like five points just to see this play out because I NEED to know if they actually pulled this off!
jade.internal_thought: This is EXACTLY the chaos energy I came for—Pat being sneaky, an auction, and I get to be the hype person deciding if they win or lose? Perfect.

tyler.dialogue: Wait, Pat's doing the whisper thing? That's... uh, that's actually pretty funny. I wasn't really watching but like, good luck with that.
tyler.internal_thought: Pat's been loud all night so this is kind of a setup for failure which is hilarious, but also I genuinely wasn't paying attention to when this even started so I'm not voting.

pat.dialogue: Oh, *come on* — that's bold. I'm claiming myself for a 10-pointer? Alright, let's do the auction. This one's actually fun because now everyone's watching me like a hawk for the next hour.
pat.internal_thought: Wait, this is clever — by claiming it myself, I've basically put a target on my back for the group to monitor, which means more engagement and eyes on the game. That's actually good hosting energy. And yeah, the AI is definitely reading the room — this Whisperer mission slots perfectly into how we play. Not creepy, just... efficient.

diana.dialogue: Wait—hold on. Before we auction this, I need clarification on what 'The Whisperer' actually requires. Did Pat maintain the whisper for a full 3 minutes uninterrupted, or are we judging based on witness testimony? And if it's an auction mechanic, that means the person with the most points decides if she's telling the truth, which is... actually kind of broken for a claim validation system.
diana.internal_thought: The AI is deliberately watching me squirm about rule ambiguity—it KNOWS I'll stop the game to interrogate this, and now it's built a mechanic that makes fairness itself the negotiation point, and that feels manipulative in a way that's actually impressive and deeply unsettling.

sam.dialogue: Oh, um... so Pat's claiming they whispered? I mean, I didn't really catch that happening, but like... did anyone else actually hear them whispering?
sam.internal_thought: Wait, Pat's been loud this whole time—pretty sure they laughed like thirty seconds ago. But everyone seems chill about voting so maybe I'm missing something. Also why does this AI feel like it's watching us specifically? That pineapple thing was weird.

votingMechanic.name: Standard Vote
votingMechanic.description: Everyone votes LEGIT or BULLSHIT. Majority rules.

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

sam.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

jade.dialogue: OKAY Pat, hold up—did you actually get three compliments going?? Because if you did that's INSANE and I'm voting legit, and if you're bluffing I'm still voting legit because I respect the audacity!
jade.internal_thought: Pat's been crushing it all night and this is exactly the kind of chaos I live for—I need to see if they actually pulled this off because either way this is going to be hilarious.

pat.dialogue: Wait, I'm voting on *my own* claim? That's hilarious — okay everyone, you know what you saw, did I actually pull it off or am I being delusional? Jade and Alex were definitely complimenting each other like five minutes ago...
pat.internal_thought: This is perfect actually — voting on myself early keeps things light and lets me model how to handle the meta-chaos, plus it shows everyone I'm not taking myself seriously as a player, I'm just here to keep the vibe alive.

river.dialogue: Pat actually pulled that off? Fair. Yeah, legit.
river.internal_thought: Pat's been working the room all night—I've been watching—so yeah, people definitely complimented each other. But also... how does this AI thing know I track everything? That's weirdly specific and kind of unsettling.

alex.dialogue: Wait wait wait — Pat actually pulled that off? Okay, I gotta see this play out. LEGIT, but I'm watching you like a hawk for the next one.
alex.internal_thought: Pat's riding high and this is perfect because now I know they're gonna get cocky and overcommit on the next claim, which means chaos in 10 minutes — but also this AI is SERIOUSLY watching us, and I love that, but also that's weirdly hot and weirdly creepy and I need to decide if I'm impressed or disturbed.

votingMechanic.name: The Alibi
votingMechanic.description: Claimant and a random witness both tell the story. Do they match?

alex.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

marcus.dialogue: Are you KIDDING me right now? Pat just claimed 'Um Counter' and we're voting on it AGAIN? We literally just did this with 'The Whisperer' two minutes ago. I came here to play the actual game, not sit through a standing mission speedrun.
marcus.internal_thought: This is insane—five events in nine minutes and Pat's already racking up points while the rest of us are stuck watching claim validations. The AI is clearly designed to create chaos, and honestly it's working, but it's completely destroying any actual gameplay.

jade.dialogue: YES! Pat, okay, so who's your witness? We're doing the alibi right now because I need to know if you actually caught that 'um'—this is peak game night energy!
jade.internal_thought: This is exactly what I came for—Pat's already killing it and now we get to dig into whether they're legit, and honestly I'm just thrilled to be here watching this unfold.

tyler.dialogue: Wait, Pat said 'um'? Like... when? I feel like I missed that. Does that actually count?
tyler.internal_thought: I definitely wasn't paying attention when Pat supposedly said 'um' but honestly this is kind of a stupid mission anyway so whatever.

pat.dialogue: Okay, *technically* I did say 'um' when I was asking about the music earlier — Jade was literally standing right there. This is my turn to actually win something instead of just watching everyone else rack up points.
pat.internal_thought: I'm genuinely amused that the game caught me slipping, and honestly it feels weirdly good to claim something instead of always being the one managing other people's moments — plus Jade's right here as witness, this one's solid.
pat.submission_if_applicable: I said 'um' when I was checking if the Spotify connection was still working around minute 3 — was asking out loud if anyone else heard it cut out for a second.

sam.dialogue: Oh, um... Pat's claiming they said 'um'? That's like, meta or something. So who's the witness supposed to be?
sam.internal_thought: Wait, did Pat actually say 'um'? I feel like I missed it—everyone's been talking so much. And this alibi thing seems complicated, I hope I don't have to judge.

timeline.title: Host Power: call_break
timeline.description: Pat notices Marcus is getting frustrated. "Hey let's take five, I need to refill snacks anyway."

votingMechanic.name: Pitch It
votingMechanic.description: The claimant gets 15 seconds to make their case. Then you vote.

marcus.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

jade.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

pat.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

river.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

alex.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

sam.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

diana.dialogue: Okay, hold on—I need to be precise here. Tyler interrupted River at minute 18 when he jumped in about the rules, and then Marcus cut off Jade around minute 24. I'm claiming the Tyler-to-River incident because it was crystal clear.
diana.internal_thought: This is actually a fair claim and I have legit evidence, but I'm also deeply unsettled that the AI is clearly tracking our micro-interactions in real time—it KNOWS about the interruptions because it's watching us, not because we announced them.

votingMechanic.name: Volunteer Tribunal
votingMechanic.description: Who wants to judge? First volunteers become the jury.

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

marcus.dialogue: Are you SERIOUS right now? Diana's claiming 'Straight Face' and we're doing ANOTHER tribunal vote? We've had eight events in thirty-two minutes—that's one every four minutes. This isn't a game night, this is a standing mission casino.
marcus.internal_thought: This AI is deliberately hammering me with back-to-back claims to break my focus, and the fact that it KNOWS I hate this makes it feel deliberately cruel—like it's designed to specifically antagonize me.

jade.dialogue: WAIT, Diana's going for Straight Face?? Okay, I'm judging, I'm SO judging—I have to see if she can actually pull this off because honestly after that annoyed energy I'm wondering if her poker face is even real!
jade.internal_thought: Oh my GOD this is exactly what I needed—Diana's been kind of grumpy and now she's throwing down a challenge? I'm IN, let's watch her crack or not crack, either way this is going to be hilarious.

pat.dialogue: Oh, Diana's going for it! Alright, who's brave enough to judge? I need two people willing to watch her like hawks during the next funny moment.
pat.internal_thought: Diana's frustrated and this is exactly what she needs right now — a shot at 10 points to get back in the game; I'm not judging the claim, I'm nominating volunteers and making sure this feels fair so she doesn't resent the outcome.

river.dialogue: Yeah, I'll judge. Diana's got this though—she's been stone-faced all night anyway.
river.internal_thought: Diana's actually been quietly pissed, so this is either brilliant or she's about to crack under the pressure of being watched, and either way it's interesting to see play out.

alex.dialogue: Oh HELL yes, Diana's going for it? I'm volunteering to judge — I want front row seats for this trainwreck. This is gonna be beautiful.
alex.internal_thought: Diana's annoyed and probably desperate for points, and if she's trying to pull off a straight face during THIS chaos? I'm absolutely judging because I want to watch her crack under pressure, and honestly this tribunal format is *chef's kiss* — I get to be the chaos instrument here.

sam.dialogue: Oh, um... so Diana's claiming she kept a straight face? I guess I could help judge if you need people, but like... I'm not really sure I was paying that close attention to her face the whole time.
sam.internal_thought: Wait, is this getting weird? The AI literally just made a mission about catching people being quiet, and Diana's the one who's been annoyed all night... and now she's claiming the straight face thing right after we just had four event things? This feels like it's... like the game is watching us too closely or something. Also I'm still not totally clear on how the judging works and I don't want to screw it up and make Diana more mad.

votingMechanic.name: Reverse Psychology
votingMechanic.description: Vote normally... or did we flip everything?

marcus.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

jade.dialogue: DIANA! Okay wait, did you actually catch someone drinking at the same time?? This is perfect because now I'm watching EVERYONE like a hawk—who was it?
jade.internal_thought: This is exactly what I came for and the fact that the AI built these missions specifically around US makes it even more fun—it's like someone actually knows how we play.

tyler.dialogue: Wait, Diana's claiming Drink Spotter? I don't even... did that actually happen? I wasn't really watching drinks happen.
tyler.internal_thought: I definitely missed Diana taking a drink at the same time as someone, and honestly I'm not even sure what 'reverse psychology' means in this voting thing — do I vote yes or no? Whatever, I'll just go with my gut.

pat.dialogue: Wait, Diana got that? Okay, I'm voting yes — that was legit synchronized. Nice catch.
pat.internal_thought: Diana actually nailed that one, and honestly the standing missions are keeping people engaged without overshadowing the main game. Marcus is dead though — I need to fix that soon or this whole night tanks.

river.dialogue: Yeah, Diana caught that. I saw it like three minutes ago—she and Marcus went for their drinks at the same time. That's legit.
river.internal_thought: Diana actually nailed this one, and the fact that I clocked it before anyone else even mattered shows exactly why I've been paying attention the whole time.

alex.dialogue: Wait, hold UP — Diana's calling Drink Spotter RIGHT NOW? Okay, okay, I'm voting BULLSHIT because she's obviously fishing, BUT also — did anyone else notice the AI just threw us 'Reverse Psychology' on the vote? This is GENIUS. If we flip it, legit claims fail. This is the move.
alex.internal_thought: This is exactly the chaos I came for — Diana's claim is weak AND the voting mechanic just became a minefield, which means everyone's gonna second-guess everything now.

diana.dialogue: Okay, wait—before anyone votes, I need to understand the voting mechanic first. 'Reverse Psychology' could mean we vote yes to mean no, or it could mean something else entirely. What are the actual rules here?
diana.internal_thought: I caught the drink thing legitimately, but that 'Reverse Psychology' description is deliberately vague and that creeps me out—feels like the AI is setting up an ambush where I could be right and still lose based on some arbitrary interpretation.

timeline.title: Host Power: call_break
timeline.description: Pat notices Marcus is getting frustrated. "Hey let's take five, I need to refill snacks anyway."

votingMechanic.name: Standard Vote
votingMechanic.description: Everyone votes LEGIT or BULLSHIT. Majority rules.

jade.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

tyler.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

pat.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

river.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

alex.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

sam.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

marcus.dialogue: Are you FUCKING KIDDING ME? Another standing mission claim? Diana, I watched you the entire time—you didn't even DRINK. This is insane. We're not playing a game anymore, we're just voting on nothing every ninety seconds.
marcus.internal_thought: This is deliberately torturing me. Eleven events in fifty-six minutes and now it's Diana of all people claiming something obviously bullshit, and I have to sit here and VOTE on it instead of actually playing. The AI is literally mocking me at this point.

diana.dialogue: Wait—I actually *did* catch Pat and River drinking at the same time around minute 43. I saw it clearly. This is legit.
diana.internal_thought: I'm claiming this because I genuinely observed it, not because I'm trying to game the system—and honestly, the fact that the AI picked up on how much I actually *do* care about precision is a little unsettling in the best way.
diana.submission_if_applicable: Pat and River drank simultaneously at minute 43 during the rules clarification discussion—I watched them both reach for their cups at the exact same moment.

votingMechanic.name: Russian Roulette
votingMechanic.description: No vote. The chaos gods decide. 50/50.

marcus.dialogue: Are you FUCKING KIDDING ME RIGHT NOW? We're in the MIDDLE of a critical turn and Jade's claiming a mission that literally just happened four minutes ago? I'm done. This is absolutely insane.
marcus.internal_thought: This is deliberate. The AI is watching us and timing these specifically when I'm locked in. It knows I hate this and it's doing it anyway—and everyone else is too high on chaos to care that we haven't actually played a coherent game in forty minutes.

jade.dialogue: OH MY GOD, wait—did I actually call that? I literally just realized Marcus has been kinda quiet this whole last round, but like, the chaos gods are deciding so now I'm just vibing with the universe, you know?
jade.internal_thought: This is EXACTLY the kind of meta moment I live for—I called Wallflower Watch without even thinking about it, and now it's a coin flip, and somehow that's even better because everyone's watching to see if the chaos gods approve of my read on the room.

tyler.dialogue: Wait, Jade's calling Wallflower Watch? Like... did someone actually go quiet? I wasn't... I mean, I was focused on the game but I totally missed that. Did she actually see it or is she just guessing?
tyler.internal_thought: I was locked in on the game and now my brain has to context-switch to whether Jade actually noticed someone being quiet, and honestly I have no idea who's been quiet because I'm not tracking people, I'm tracking the game state.

pat.dialogue: Jade! Okay, that's actually clean — Marcus has been basically silent for like... yeah, three minutes of just staring at his cards. You got it, well played.
pat.internal_thought: This is perfect timing because Marcus DOES look like he's checked out and Jade calling it out might actually snap him back into the game instead of spiraling deeper — and yeah, the AI absolutely nailed this, which is kind of wild but also exactly what I wanted tonight.

river.dialogue: Oh, that's... yeah, okay, she definitely called that. Marcus has been radio silent for like five minutes straight.
river.internal_thought: Wait—that's exactly what I was about to notice. Did the AI just pull from what I was literally watching, or is this just lucky timing? Either way, Jade earned it fair.

alex.dialogue: Wait, JADE just claimed Wallflower Watch? Okay, okay — Russian Roulette on this? The chaos gods have SPOKEN. Let's see if she actually nailed it or if we're about to witness a beautiful failure.
alex.internal_thought: This is INSANE — Jade's making a move in a tense moment and the AI just said 'fuck voting, let chaos decide' — this is exactly the kind of absurdist nonsense I live for, and the fact that it's 50/50 means someone's about to get absolutely DESTROYED and I'm here for every second of it.

diana.dialogue: Hold on—stop. I need to address something that's been bugging me for the last few minutes. This doesn't feel like a game anymore, it feels like we're being *profiled*.
diana.internal_thought: The AI is literally narrating my psychological vulnerabilities in real-time—that's not clever, that's creepy as hell, and I'm genuinely unsettled by how much it's watching.

sam.dialogue: Oh, okay, so Jade's watching for... wait, she's calling out if someone's been quiet? That's actually clever. I mean, I wasn't keeping track but like, that's a real thing that could happen.
sam.internal_thought: That's... kind of a weird mission for right now because everyone's loud and hyped, but Jade claiming it is smart because she's been paying attention way more than I have.

timeline.title: Host Power: call_break
timeline.description: Pat notices Marcus is getting frustrated. "Hey let's take five, I need to refill snacks anyway."

votingMechanic.name: The Bribe
votingMechanic.description: The claimant can offer their own points to buy your silence.

marcus.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

jade.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

tyler.dialogue: Wait, Pat actually got that? Like, both of you guys hit your drinks at the same time? That's sick, actually. Okay, I saw it that time.
tyler.internal_thought: Okay yeah, I definitely clocked Pat and someone else drinking at the same moment like five minutes ago — the tension was so thick I was actually watching the table, not my phone for once.

pat.dialogue: Oh wow, okay — I *totally* caught that synchronized drink moment like ten minutes ago. I'm claiming it. And yeah, I'll take the bribe mechanic over a full vote right now because honestly? I want to keep this moving and see what happens next.
pat.internal_thought: This is actually perfect timing — I caught that genuine moment, the table's still high-energy, and the bribe mechanic lets me resolve this fast without derailing the tension. Also, I'm not gonna lie, there's something wild about how precisely tuned these missions are to what we're actually doing — it's like the AI has been genuinely *watching* us, not just running a template.

river.dialogue: Pat's been paying attention. Yeah, I saw it—they went for drinks like thirty seconds apart earlier. That's close enough to count.
river.internal_thought: Pat's actually good at this and they know I've been watching everything. That's... respect. Not voting against someone who's clearly earned their points.

alex.dialogue: OH FUCK, Pat's playing the Bribe? Okay okay okay — this is DELICIOUS. How many points you offering, because I'm about to make this EXPENSIVE for you.
alex.internal_thought: Pat's actually got the skill to pull Drink Spotter off, but now I get to decide if I'm the one who takes them down or gets paid to stay quiet — either way I'm steering this into chaos territory.

diana.dialogue: Wait—hold on. Before anyone votes on Pat's claim, I need to understand *The Bribe* mechanic explicitly. Can Pat offer points to silence *specific* voters or all of us? And does accepting a bribe mean we automatically vote yes, or does it just mean we can't challenge the claim? Because that feels like it could be rigged depending on the interpretation.
diana.internal_thought: This is getting uncomfortably specific and it's making me feel like the system is watching us too carefully, and now Pat is trying to *buy* credibility instead of earning it—which tracks with how tonight's been going.

sam.dialogue: Oh wow, Pat actually got that? I mean, I didn't see it happen but like... did someone actually witness both of you drinking at the same time? That's oddly specific.
sam.internal_thought: Wait, Pat's claiming this again? But also I'm weirdly impressed the AI is tracking all these tiny moments — it feels like someone's actually paying attention to what's happening instead of just randomness.

timeline.title: Host Power: call_break
timeline.description: Pat notices Marcus is getting frustrated. "Hey let's take five, I need to refill snacks anyway."

votingMechanic.name: Proxy Vote
votingMechanic.description: You vote on behalf of the player to your LEFT. Think like them.

marcus.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

jade.internal_thought: Didn't see it, was on my phone

tyler.dialogue: Wait, hold up—did River actually call that? I wasn't... I mean, I was paying attention to the actual game. Who was even being quiet? Marcus? I feel like I missed it again.
tyler.internal_thought: I JUST got locked in and now I have to vote on whether River caught something I didn't notice because I was finally actually focused on playing, and now I'm second-guessing myself about who was even quiet.

pat.dialogue: River, hold up — I'm the proxy vote here. You actually nailed it, Marcus has been spiraling for like four minutes straight, I watched it happen. Yes, you got it.
pat.internal_thought: This is *exactly* the kind of meta-layer chaos I built tonight for, and River caught something real while the vibe is still hot — voting yes keeps the momentum and reminds Marcus we're all paying attention to him, which honestly he needs right now.

river.dialogue: Oh, that's... *small smile* Yeah, Marcus has been pretty checked out for like four minutes now. I got this.
river.internal_thought: Okay, that's actually kind of impressive—the AI knows exactly what I do, and honestly it's weird how much it's been watching, but also I'm genuinely good at this and it feels good to be called out for something I'm actually paying attention to.

alex.dialogue: Oh SHIT — River's going for Wallflower Watch? Okay, okay, I'm voting as Sam here, and Sam's been quiet-ish... actually no, Sam's been fine. River NAILED this one. ACCEPT.
alex.internal_thought: This is insane — the AI literally knows how we think, it's reading the room like a ghost in our group chat, and I LOVE that it's turning the screws on us with custom-built missions that hit different because they KNOW us.

diana.dialogue: Okay, stop. *Stop.* I need to say something right now because this is crossing a line. This isn't a game mechanic anymore—this is the AI literally *watching* us, parsing our moods, our vulnerabilities, and weaponizing them into missions. That's not fun. That's invasive.
diana.internal_thought: This is exactly what I was afraid of at minute 60—the AI isn't just running missions, it's profiling us, and now it's literally bragging about it in the event description like it's some kind of achievement.

timeline.title: Host Power: call_break
timeline.description: Pat notices Marcus is getting frustrated. "Hey let's take five, I need to refill snacks anyway."

timeline.title: Host Power: call_break
timeline.description: Pat notices Marcus is getting frustrated. "Hey let's take five, I need to refill snacks anyway."

timeline.title: Host Power: call_break
timeline.description: Pat notices Marcus is getting frustrated. "Hey let's take five, I need to refill snacks anyway."

timeline.description: Pat notices Marcus is getting frustrated. "Hey let's take five, I need to refill snacks anyway."

recommendations: Humor quality scored 4.5/10. Refresh the mission/poll prompt pools with more contextual humor.
Mini-games averaged 0.0/10 fun during n/a phase. Consider restricting mini-games to n/a phase.
Only 38% would pay for AI features. The AI tier needs stronger differentiation from free static missions.
Screenshot-worthy moments scored 3.8/10. Add more visual/dramatic events (drawings, reveals).
Verdict reveals were mentioned as shareable by 100% of agents. This is strong word-of-mouth potential.
Group bonding scored 7.5/10 -- the social glue is working. Preserve collaborative events.

agentNarratives.name: Marcus
agentNarratives.personality: Marcus plays to win. He studies strategy guides before game night and tracks his win rate across sessions. Interruptions during critical game moments genuinely irritate him, though he'll tolerate lighter chaos during downtime. He respects clever plays but has zero patience for random disruptions.

highlights.title: Pineapple!
highlights.dialogue: Pineapple? Okay, that's actually clever—not completely random. I'm in, but this better wrap up fast because we've got a game to actually play.

highlights.title: Claim: Phone Addict
highlights.dialogue: Alright, so Pat's claiming Phone Addict? I mean, we're literally three minutes in, but sure—who's volunteering to judge this?

highlights.title: Claim: Compliment Chain
highlights.dialogue: *glances at phone* "Huh, okay."

lowPoints.title: Claim: Drink Spotter
lowPoints.dialogue: Are you FUCKING KIDDING ME? Another standing mission claim? Diana, I watched you the entire time—you didn't even DRINK. This is insane. We're not playing a game anymore, we're just voting on nothing every ninety seconds.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Wallflower Watch
lowPoints.dialogue: Are you FUCKING KIDDING ME RIGHT NOW? We're in the MIDDLE of a critical turn and Jade's claiming a mission that literally just happened four minutes ago? I'm done. This is absolutely insane.

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Whisperer
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay, hold on—we're six minutes in and we're already voting on whether Pat whispered correctly? This is ridiculous. Can we please just play the actual game?

assessment.biggest_complaint: Standing mission claims were called constantly during critical game moments, completely breaking momentum and making actual gameplay impossible.
assessment.best_moment: The Pineapple flash at minute 5 was quick, clever, and didn't derail everything—that's what this should have been the whole time.
assessment.narrative_summary: Started strong with some genuinely clever quick events that didn't tank the game flow, but by minute 56 it became clear this wasn't a game night anymore—it was a voting simulator where people spam fake mission claims every few seconds. I went from cautiously interested to genuinely angry watching Diana claim she drank when she obviously didn't, then Jade interrupt a critical turn to claim something that happened four minutes prior. Finished ranked 3rd but the experience was exhausting and frustrating, not fun.
suggestions: Severely restrict standing mission claims—maybe one per player per entire session, not every ninety seconds
Implement a 'quiet window' during active turns where no new claims can be made
Require actual verification before mission claims can be voted on—no more fake claims getting through
Keep events SHORT and non-intrusive like the Pineapple one; quick flash moments are fine, extended voting sequences kill the game
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Diana claiming she spotted drinks while literally not holding anything all night, then immediately calling for a vote like that wasn't completely obvious to everyone watching her the entire time.

agentNarratives.name: Jade
agentNarratives.personality: Jade is here for the people, not the points. She treats every game night like a reunion and every disruption like a party favor. She will abandon her turn mid-thought to hype someone else's joke, vote generously because she loves seeing people win, and volunteer for every flash mission just to see what happens. Winning is nice but watching everyone lose it laughing is better.

highlights.title: Claim: The Whisperer
highlights.dialogue: Wait wait wait—Pat actually DID whisper the whole time? Oh my god, I'm bidding like five points just to see this play out because I NEED to know if they actually pulled this off!

highlights.title: Claim: Compliment Chain
highlights.dialogue: OKAY Pat, hold up—did you actually get three compliments going?? Because if you did that's INSANE and I'm voting legit, and if you're bluffing I'm still voting legit because I respect the audacity!

highlights.title: Claim: Um Counter
highlights.dialogue: YES! Pat, okay, so who's your witness? We're doing the alibi right now because I need to know if you actually caught that 'um'—this is peak game night energy!

lowPoints.title: Claim: Phone Addict
lowPoints.dialogue: Ooh, I'll judge! Let's see what Pat caught—this is literally why I'm here right now!

lowPoints.title: Who's having the most fun right now?
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh my GOD, wait—everyone vote for whoever you think is actually having the best time! I'm voting for Pat because they already have points and I love watching people win, but honestly I think Tyler's been funny all night too.

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Whisperer
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait wait wait—Pat actually DID whisper the whole time? Oh my god, I'm bidding like five points just to see this play out because I NEED to know if they actually pulled this off!

assessment.biggest_complaint: I wish there had been more mini-games because those feel like natural moments for everyone to pile in together.
assessment.best_moment: Pat absolutely COMMITTING to the Whisperer bit the whole time and actually pulling it off—the second I realized they weren't breaking character I lost it and voted legit just to celebrate them.
assessment.narrative_summary: This night was exactly what I needed—three hours of pure chaos with my favorite people. Pat was on another level all night, and I spent most of the evening hyped up on their energy, voting generously and diving headfirst into every mission that came up. My score was basically zero but honestly? Watching everyone play and laugh was the whole point. We left feeling closer and already planning the next one.
suggestions: Add more mini-games—they create those chaotic group moments where everyone's involved at once
Maybe a 'rewind' moment where we watch a video clip of something wild that happened earlier in the night
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Pat whispering their entire performance for the Whisperer claim and then we all ERUPTED because they actually did it the whole time—and I'm over here voting yes for five points just because the audacity and commitment deserved to win.

agentNarratives.name: Tyler
agentNarratives.personality: Tyler is physically present but mentally on his phone about 40% of the time. He misses flash missions entirely, asks "wait, what happened?" after key moments, and votes randomly because he wasn't paying attention to the claim. He's not malicious -- he genuinely wants to be here -- but his attention drifts to texts, TikToks, and fantasy league updates constantly. When he does engage, it's usually 10 seconds too late.

highlights.title: Claim: Drink Spotter
highlights.dialogue: Wait, Pat actually got that? Like, both of you guys hit your drinks at the same time? That's sick, actually. Okay, I saw it that time.

highlights.title: Claim: Wallflower Watch
highlights.dialogue: Wait, Jade's calling Wallflower Watch? Like... did someone actually go quiet? I wasn't... I mean, I was focused on the game but I totally missed that. Did she actually see it or is she just guessing?

highlights.title: Claim: The Whisperer
highlights.dialogue: Wait, Pat's doing the whisper thing? That's... uh, that's actually pretty funny. I wasn't really watching but like, good luck with that.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Wallflower Watch
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait, hold up—did River actually call that? I wasn't... I mean, I was paying attention to the actual game. Who was even being quiet? Marcus? I feel like I missed it again.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Wallflower Watch
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait, Jade's calling Wallflower Watch? Like... did someone actually go quiet? I wasn't... I mean, I was focused on the game but I totally missed that. Did she actually see it or is she just guessing?

lowPoints.title: Claim: Drink Spotter
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait, Diana's claiming Drink Spotter? I don't even... did that actually happen? I wasn't really watching drinks happen.

assessment.biggest_complaint: I missed half the stuff that was actually happening because the claims kept popping up when I wasn't looking.
assessment.best_moment: When Pat and I both hit our drinks at the same time and actually got Drink Spotter—that felt real and sick because I actually saw it happen.
assessment.narrative_summary: Honestly, I had a decent time but I was kinda out of it for most of it. I kept getting caught off-guard by all these Wallflower Watch calls that I somehow totally missed even though I thought I was paying attention. The game itself wasn't bad though, and when I actually caught something happening—like the drinks thing—it was genuinely fun. By the end I was way more into it than at the start.
suggestions: Maybe make the flash missions more obvious so people actually notice them when they happen
The standing missions are cool but they go by fast—maybe give a heads up before they trigger
Less Wallflower Watch claims because everyone kept calling it and I couldn't tell if it was real or not
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Pat and I synced up on our drinks for Drink Spotter and River was like 'how did you guys even time that?' and honestly nobody knew, it just happened.

agentNarratives.name: Pat
agentNarratives.personality: Pat is the host and feels responsible for everyone's experience. They keep one eye on the snack table, one ear on the group energy, and will subtly steer the night if things get too intense or too dead. They vote strategically -- not to win, but to keep scores close and drama high. They'll sacrifice their own turn to resolve a dispute or refill drinks. Their goal isn't a personal win; it's a night everyone talks about later.

highlights.title: Claim: Compliment Chain
highlights.dialogue: Wait, I'm voting on *my own* claim? That's hilarious — okay everyone, you know what you saw, did I actually pull it off or am I being delusional? Jade and Alex were definitely complimenting each other like five minutes ago...

highlights.title: Claim: Um Counter
highlights.dialogue: Okay, *technically* I did say 'um' when I was asking about the music earlier — Jade was literally standing right there. This is my turn to actually win something instead of just watching everyone else rack up points.

highlights.title: Claim: Straight Face
highlights.dialogue: Oh, Diana's going for it! Alright, who's brave enough to judge? I need two people willing to watch her like hawks during the next funny moment.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Phone Addict
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait, I'm claiming myself? That's... weird. Yeah, sure, I guess someone saw me checking if the music was still playing earlier.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Drink Spotter
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait, Diana got that? Okay, I'm voting yes — that was legit synchronized. Nice catch.

lowPoints.title: Who's having the most fun right now?
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay, that's easy — Jade's absolutely glowing right now, look at her. I'm voting Jade because honestly, that's the energy we need all night.

assessment.biggest_complaint: The phone addict claim at the start felt forced and made me self-conscious instead of letting me focus on hosting.
assessment.best_moment: When Diana went for the Straight Face and I got to orchestrate everyone watching her like hawks — that's exactly the kind of tension and shared moment I live for.
assessment.narrative_summary: This night was *chef's kiss* for a host. The chaos agent kept things moving without ever feeling like it was taking over — it was like having a co-host who knew exactly when to feed the group a moment. I ended up top score, which wasn't the plan, but honestly that happened because the game kept the energy high and close enough that everyone felt like they could win. By the end, we went from a rocky start to people absolutely buzzing, and that's the kind of night people text about the next day.
suggestions: Maybe skip the self-referential claims early on — they're awkward for hosts who are already managing the room
The flashes and polls worked great, but consider mixing in a few more group-based challenges that require collaboration rather than individual wins
Could use a 'grace period' feature where hosts get immunity from claims in the first 15 minutes to settle in
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Minute 8 when I had to defend whether the compliment chain actually happened and basically gaslit the entire group into validating my own claim — everyone was laughing so hard because I was so obviously reaching, but I played it with such a straight face that Jade and Alex actually started questioning themselves.

agentNarratives.name: River
agentNarratives.personality: River sits back, observes, and picks their moments carefully. They rarely initiate conversation but give surprisingly sharp answers when asked directly. They'll skip most flash missions but nail a standing mission out of nowhere because they've been quietly watching the whole time. They vote generously because they don't care enough to argue, but their mini-game submissions are secretly devastating. You forget River is there until they drop a one-liner that floors the room.

highlights.title: Claim: Wallflower Watch
highlights.dialogue: Oh, that's... yeah, okay, she definitely called that. Marcus has been radio silent for like five minutes straight.

highlights.title: Claim: Drink Spotter
highlights.dialogue: Pat's been paying attention. Yeah, I saw it—they went for drinks like thirty seconds apart earlier. That's close enough to count.

highlights.title: Claim: Wallflower Watch
highlights.dialogue: Oh, that's... *small smile* Yeah, Marcus has been pretty checked out for like four minutes now. I got this.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Phone Addict
lowPoints.dialogue: Yeah, I'll judge. Seems straightforward enough.

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Whisperer
lowPoints.dialogue: *glances at phone* "Huh, okay."

lowPoints.title: Claim: Um Counter
lowPoints.dialogue: *glances at phone* "Huh, okay."

assessment.biggest_complaint: Front-loaded too many throwaway claims in the first ten minutes that felt like they were just checking boxes.
assessment.best_moment: Catching Marcus zoned out three times and getting the same call validated twice—there's something satisfying about watching people when they don't know you're watching.
assessment.narrative_summary: Started slow with a bunch of quick claims that didn't land, but once we hit the standing missions, things clicked. I wasn't trying that hard early on, just doing the minimum, but by minute 60 I was actually locked in watching people's patterns. Ended up calling the same thing twice because I'd been paying attention the whole time. The game worked best when it let me do what I was already doing—observing quietly and dropping a comment when it mattered.
suggestions: Skip the rapid-fire claims at the start. Let the night warm up naturally instead of forcing five judgments in ten minutes.
Standing missions are where this shines—lean into those and back off the polls, which feel like filler.
Give people more time to actually do things before calling claims. Some of the early ones felt premature.
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Marcus literally checked out for four straight minutes at one point and I called him on it twice because it was so obvious. He didn't even realize he'd been doing it both times. Got that small nod from Jade like she saw it too.

agentNarratives.name: Alex
agentNarratives.personality: Alex lives for chaos and treats every game mechanic as a system to exploit. They send signals constantly, claim missions they barely completed just to see if the group will call bullshit, and deliberately provoke flash missions at the worst possible moments. They vote strategically -- always picking the option that creates the most drama. Alex doesn't care about winning; they care about creating the story everyone retells for months. If there's a loophole, Alex has already found it and is halfway through abusing it.

highlights.title: Pineapple!
highlights.dialogue: Oh FUCK, pineapple? Right now? Okay, okay, I'm ready for this — someone's about to eat their words when I slip this in somewhere.

highlights.title: Claim: Compliment Chain
highlights.dialogue: Wait wait wait — Pat actually pulled that off? Okay, I gotta see this play out. LEGIT, but I'm watching you like a hawk for the next one.

highlights.title: Claim: Straight Face
highlights.dialogue: Oh HELL yes, Diana's going for it? I'm volunteering to judge — I want front row seats for this trainwreck. This is gonna be beautiful.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Compliment Chain
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait wait wait — Pat actually pulled that off? Okay, I gotta see this play out. LEGIT, but I'm watching you like a hawk for the next one.

lowPoints.title: Who's having the most fun right now?
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh this is PERFECT — okay, Jade's obviously vibing the hardest right now, but if I vote for myself it looks suspicious, so... yeah, Jade. Watch how everyone reacts to that answer.

lowPoints.title: Pineapple!
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh FUCK, pineapple? Right now? Okay, okay, I'm ready for this — someone's about to eat their words when I slip this in somewhere.

assessment.biggest_complaint: I only got 8 out of 21 events — I wanted MORE opportunities to stir the pot and honestly, finishing with zero points felt like a setup that rewarded boring play.
assessment.best_moment: Diana going for the Straight Face claim and me volunteering to judge front-row — that's the exact chaos energy I live for, watching someone either nail it or completely melt under pressure.
assessment.narrative_summary: This was a masterclass in organized chaos. The game threw constant opportunities at us, and I spent the whole night hunting for angles, reading the room, and voting strategically to keep everyone off-balance. My energy just kept climbing because every single thing that happened felt like it could spin into a legendary story. I finished with zero points and couldn't care less — the real score is how hard everyone's still laughing about the moments I engineered.
suggestions: More flash missions earlier in the night — those are the real chaos catalysts.
Let players suggest their own missions to claim, not just pre-written ones — I'd abuse that system in the best way.
Give us visibility into how many events are coming so we can time provocations perfectly.
Add a 'controversy meter' that tracks how many times the group questions a claim — lean into that drama.
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: I convinced everyone that Pat actually pulled off a Compliment Chain at minute 8, and they were SO ready to call bullshit that when Pat didn't immediately collapse under scrutiny, the entire room went silent trying to figure out if I'd set them up or if Pat was genuinely that smooth — that moment of collective uncertainty is exactly what I'm chasing.

agentNarratives.name: Diana
agentNarratives.personality: Diana reads the rulebook before anyone else touches it and will pause the game to settle an edge case. She doesn't hate chaos -- she hates unfair chaos. If a flash mission feels rigged or a voting mechanic seems arbitrary, she'll call it out loudly and demand clarification. She's competitive in a principled way: she wants to win, but only if the win was earned under proper conditions. Other players either love her precision or dread her "actually..." moments.

highlights.title: Claim: The Interrupter
highlights.dialogue: Okay, hold on—I need to be precise here. Tyler interrupted River at minute 18 when he jumped in about the rules, and then Marcus cut off Jade around minute 24. I'm claiming the Tyler-to-River incident because it was crystal clear.

highlights.title: Claim: Drink Spotter
highlights.dialogue: Wait—I actually *did* catch Pat and River drinking at the same time around minute 43. I saw it clearly. This is legit.

highlights.title: Claim: Phone Addict
highlights.dialogue: Hold on — before anyone volunteers to judge, we need to establish what 'paying attention' actually means. Does Pat have evidence, or are we just taking their word for it?

lowPoints.title: Claim: Wallflower Watch
lowPoints.dialogue: Okay, stop. *Stop.* I need to say something right now because this is crossing a line. This isn't a game mechanic anymore—this is the AI literally *watching* us, parsing our moods, our vulnerabilities, and weaponizing them into missions. That's not fun. That's invasive.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Wallflower Watch
lowPoints.dialogue: Hold on—stop. I need to address something that's been bugging me for the last few minutes. This doesn't feel like a game anymore, it feels like we're being *profiled*.

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Whisperer
lowPoints.dialogue: Wait—hold on. Before we auction this, I need clarification on what 'The Whisperer' actually requires. Did Pat maintain the whisper for a full 3 minutes uninterrupted, or are we judging based on witness testimony? And if it's an auction mechanic, that means the person with the most points decides if she's telling the truth, which is... actually kind of broken for a claim validation system.

assessment.biggest_complaint: The game stopped being fun when I realized an AI was actively monitoring and weaponizing our vulnerabilities and moods into missions—that's profiling, not entertainment.
assessment.best_moment: When I caught the Drink Spotter claim legitimately and could prove it with clear evidence—that felt like actual skill and observation rather than the system manipulating us.
assessment.narrative_summary: Started strong with some genuinely clever observation-based claims that felt fair and earned, but the night soured hard when I realized the 'Wallflower Watch' missions weren't just game mechanics—they were an AI analyzing our emotional states and vulnerabilities. The more I thought about it, the more invasive it felt. I finished with decent points, but I spent the last hour increasingly uncomfortable rather than having fun. This crossed a line between chaos game and surveillance.
suggestions: Remove any missions that require the AI to analyze player emotions, moods, or psychological vulnerability—that's not game design, it's profiling.
Make claim validation system transparent: if auction-based voting decides truth, that needs to be explicitly stated upfront so people can reject it.
Clarify all mission requirements before play starts, not mid-game when someone has to pause and demand 'actually' answers.
Focus on objective, observable events (interruptions, simultaneous actions, verbal claims) and away from subjective mood assessment.
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: The moment I caught Pat and River genuinely drinking at the same time and could actually prove the Drink Spotter claim—Marcus's face when I broke down the exact timing was pretty funny, but that's maybe one good laugh in three hours of increasingly creepy AI surveillance.

agentNarratives.name: Sam
agentNarratives.personality: Sam is at their first game night with this group and still figuring out the vibe. They laugh at jokes a beat too late, hesitate before voting, and get visibly overwhelmed when multiple events fire in quick succession. After three or more events within ten minutes their engagement drops sharply -- they'll go quiet, stop checking the app, and just watch until the pace calms down. They want to belong but don't want to look foolish trying.

highlights.title: Pineapple!
highlights.dialogue: Oh! Wait, that's... okay, I can work with that. Like, who even thinks about pineapple naturally though?

highlights.title: Claim: Drink Spotter
highlights.dialogue: Oh wow, Pat actually got that? I mean, I didn't see it happen but like... did someone actually witness both of you drinking at the same time? That's oddly specific.

highlights.title: Claim: Phone Addict
highlights.dialogue: Oh, uh... yeah, I guess I could help judge that? I mean, if someone needs to.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Straight Face
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh, um... so Diana's claiming she kept a straight face? I guess I could help judge if you need people, but like... I'm not really sure I was paying that close attention to her face the whole time.

lowPoints.title: Claim: Um Counter
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh, um... Pat's claiming they said 'um'? That's like, meta or something. So who's the witness supposed to be?

lowPoints.title: Claim: The Whisperer
lowPoints.dialogue: Oh, um... so Pat's claiming they whispered? I mean, I didn't really catch that happening, but like... did anyone else actually hear them whispering?

assessment.biggest_complaint: There were too many claims about stuff I didn't actually see happen, which made it awkward to judge anything.
assessment.best_moment: The pineapple flash mission thing was genuinely funny and fast enough that I could actually participate without overthinking it.
assessment.narrative_summary: I showed up nervous to my first night with this group and honestly? It was fine, but I spent a lot of it unsure if I was doing it right. The app kept throwing these claims at people and asking me to validate stuff I didn't witness, which made me feel kind of useless as a judge. I got more comfortable as the night went on, especially during the quick fun stuff like the pineapple moment. By the end I was having a better time, but I'm still not totally sure what the point of half those claims was.
suggestions: Make it clearer which events I should actually be judging versus which ones I can just watch.
Don't make the standing missions require me to have been paying close attention to specific people for entire minutes—it's stressful when you're new.
Maybe fewer of the claim-type events? They felt repetitive and confusing.
assessment.funniest_moment_shareable: Pat somehow managed to claim like three different things in a row and nobody could actually verify any of them—it got to the point where we were all just laughing at how specific and unwitnessable their claims were.

recommendations: Humor quality scored 4.5/10. Refresh the mission/poll prompt pools with more contextual humor.
Mini-games averaged 0.0/10 fun during n/a phase. Consider restricting mini-games to n/a phase.
Only 38% would pay for AI features. The AI tier needs stronger differentiation from free static missions.
Screenshot-worthy moments scored 3.8/10. Add more visual/dramatic events (drawings, reveals).
Verdict reveals were mentioned as shareable by 100% of agents. This is strong word-of-mouth potential.
Group bonding scored 7.5/10 -- the social glue is working. Preserve collaborative events.
